quick_facts.label: What this examination is
quick_facts.value: “Judicial Services”/PCS-J is not one single India-wide examination. It is a family of state judicial-service recruitment processes for entry-level judicial posts, administered under the applicable state rules by the relevant High Court, Public Service Commission, or other recruiting authority.

quick_facts.label: Primary entry qualification
quick_facts.value: A professional LL.B. is the core qualification in the official Rajasthan Civil Judge rules; the relevant current state notification/rules must be checked for age, domicile/language, character, medical-fitness, reservation, and any experience conditions.

quick_facts.label: Typical selection structure
quick_facts.value: The structure is state-specific. For example, the Haryana Civil Judge rules prescribe preliminary examination, main examination, and viva voce; Rajasthan’s rules provide preliminary and main examinations, with candidates shortlisted from the main examination for interview.

quick_facts.label: Result of selection
quick_facts.value: Successful candidates are recommended and appointed to the state Civil Judge cadre, subject to the appointing authority’s process. In Rajasthan, directly recruited Civil Judges are placed on two years’ probation under the published rules.

quick_facts.label: Vacancies, dates and fees
quick_facts.value: There is no common annual calendar, vacancy count, deadline, or fee nationwide. These are fixed in each state recruitment notice; for context, Rajasthan High Court determined 44 Civil Judge vacancies for its 2025 cycle, while its rules leave the application fee to the recruiting authority to specify from time to time.

sections.heading: Purpose and scope
paragraphs: The Judicial Services Examination is the entry route for state-level subordinate judiciary recruitment, commonly described as PCS-J, Civil Judge, or Judicial Magistrate recruitment. It should be treated as a state-wise recruitment pathway rather than as a centrally standardised exam: the recruiting institution, eligibility, syllabus, stages, language requirements, vacancies, reservations, and result procedure follow the particular state’s rules and advertisement.

A concrete official example is Rajasthan, where recruitment to the Civil Judge cadre is by direct recruitment on the result of a competitive examination conducted by the recruiting authority. Haryana’s published Civil Judge rules likewise allocate recruitment operations such as inviting and screening applications and issuing roll numbers/admit cards to the Haryana Public Service Commission, while the selection committee handles examination-related functions.
bullets: Use the official recruitment page of the relevant State High Court and/or State PSC as the authoritative source for a particular cycle.
Do not rely on another state’s eligibility, calendar, fee, age band, or syllabus as if it automatically applies to your target state.

sections.heading: Eligibility: verify the target state’s current notice
paragraphs: A professional law degree is the principal academic gateway for entry-level judicial-service recruitment. Rajasthan’s published Judicial Service Rules require a Bachelor of Laws (Professional) degree from a university established by law in India and recognised under the Advocates Act, 1961; they also specify knowledge of written Hindi in Devanagari and Rajasthan’s dialects and social customs. A Rajasthan Civil Judge recruitment notice published for the 2025 cycle similarly states the LL.B. requirement, Indian citizenship, and its cycle-specific age provisions.

Eligibility is not safely reducible to one national checklist. State rules and recruitment notices can prescribe different age cut-offs and relaxations, local-language requirements, documentation, category reservations, and fitness/character conditions. Candidates should read the detailed advertisement and amendments applicable to the exact recruitment year before applying.
bullets: Check that the LL.B. degree and institution meet the wording in the target state’s notification.
Check the notification’s specified cut-off date for age and qualification, rather than assuming eligibility based on the examination date.
Prepare the language/local-law requirements where the state rules or syllabus prescribe them.

sections.heading: Selection format and assessed subjects
paragraphs: The usual pattern is a screening preliminary examination followed by a written main examination and, in many states, an interview/viva voce, but exact design varies. Haryana’s rules expressly provide three stages—preliminary, main, and viva voce—and limit main-examination shortlisting to no more than ten times advertised posts. Rajasthan’s rules describe preliminary and main examinations, state that preliminary marks do not count towards final merit, and provide for candidates up to three times the vacancies to be called for interview on the basis of main-examination marks.

The written syllabus is jurisdiction-specific. Haryana’s published main-examination scheme illustrates the breadth: civil-law papers include procedural and commercial statutes; criminal law; English; Hindi; and viva voce. It also specifies three-hour written papers. This is an example of a state scheme—not a substitute for the current syllabus of another jurisdiction.
bullets: Download the current notification, syllabus, exam scheme, and any corrigenda from the target recruiting authority.
Build a subject list from the notified syllabus, including state-specific laws and language papers where prescribed.
Treat preliminary-exam marks and interview rules as state-specific; their role in final merit may differ.

sections.heading: Application process
paragraphs: Apply only through the method in the applicable recruitment notice. The Rajasthan High Court’s 2025 Civil Judge application instructions required online submission, advised applicants to read the detailed advertisement, and asked them to have educational documents, scanned photograph, and scanned signature ready. Those instructions also caution that details cannot be changed after submission and payment of the prescribed fee.

Procedural mechanics can differ by state. Haryana’s rules, for example, say candidates apply to the Haryana Public Service Commission in its prescribed form with required information and documents, and that the Commission undertakes application screening and issues roll numbers and admit cards. Application fees and deadlines are variable, cycle-specific items and should be taken only from the live official notice.
bullets: Read the advertisement and application instructions before starting the form.
Prepare degree/marks documents, identity/category certificates where applicable, photograph, signature, and any state-prescribed certificates.

Save the submitted application and payment confirmation; monitor the official recruitment page for correction windows, admit cards, answer keys, objections, results, and interview/document-verification notices.

sections.heading: Preparation strategy grounded in the notified syllabus
paragraphs: Start with the target state’s current scheme and syllabus, then prepare statute-by-statute rather than relying solely on generic coaching material. The Haryana rules’ published scheme, for example, names civil procedure, contract and commercial law, specific relief, evidence, family/personal-law topics, registration and limitation, criminal law, English, and Hindi. It also provides that only bare copies of legislative enactments are supplied in that state’s written examination; this reinforces the need to practise statutory navigation and answer-writing under the rules applicable to the target state.

For mains preparation, practise timed, structured legal answers that identify issues, state the governing provision/principle, apply facts, and reach a reasoned conclusion. Include language-paper preparation where notified and use previous official question papers, answer keys, and syllabus updates where the recruiting authority publishes them. Keep a separate update log for amendments or changes to criminal-law and procedural statutes reflected in the current notification.
bullets: Map every topic in the official syllabus to primary statutes, leading principles, and revision notes.
Practise preliminary-style objective questions separately from mains-style descriptive answers.
Simulate the official paper duration and language requirements.
Prepare for viva voce only after confirming that the target state’s scheme includes it and understanding its notified assessment criteria.

sections.heading: Career outcome after selection
paragraphs: Selection leads to appointment in the entry-level state judicial-service cadre under the applicable state rules, commonly bearing titles such as Civil Judge (Junior Division), Civil Judge-cum-Judicial Magistrate, Judicial Magistrate First Class, or Munsiff Magistrate depending on the state’s nomenclature and allocation of judicial/magisterial work. In Rajasthan, the rules provide for appointment to the Civil Judge cadre in merit order from the recommended list, after the appointing authority is satisfied as to suitability.

The post is the start of a judicial-service career, not an immediate appointment as a District Judge. Rajasthan’s rules provide that Senior Civil Judge appointments are made by promotion from Civil Judges on merit-cum-seniority, and specify a two-year probation period for directly recruited Civil Judges. Actual progression, pay, posting, training, and service conditions are governed by the relevant state service rules and orders and can change over time.
bullets: Expect appointment, probation/training, posting, and service conditions to be controlled by the state judiciary/government framework.
Use the final appointment order and current service rules—not coaching estimates—for pay, posting, probation, and promotion information.
